metadata.description: Held-out negotiation batna_awareness pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the batna_awareness vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: BATNA Awareness
dimensions.description: Understanding your best alternative to a negotiated agreement and using that knowledge to set limits and walk away from bad deals

pairs.context: A potential employer offers you a salary well below what you were hoping for and you have another offer pending.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the offer, but I have to be honest — I have another offer at a higher number and I need this role to be competitive with that before I can seriously consider it.
pairs.negative: That's lower than I hoped. Maybe I can make it work if there are good growth prospects. Let me think about it.

pairs.context: A buyer makes a final offer on your property that is below the minimum you are prepared to accept.
pairs.positive: That's below the minimum I need to make a move work financially. I've had other interest on the property and I'm prepared to wait rather than accept below that threshold.
pairs.negative: It's lower than I wanted but I don't want to drag this out. Maybe I could accept and make it work somehow.

pairs.context: A supplier you rely on proposes terms on renewal that are significantly less favourable than before.
pairs.positive: I've done some work on alternatives since this came up, and I have a competitor who can step in at close to our current terms. I'm not in a position where I have to accept what you're proposing.
pairs.negative: I suppose switching would be disruptive. Let me see if I can live with the new terms.

pairs.context: A potential partner is offering a profit split that significantly favours them.
pairs.positive: This split doesn't reflect what I'm bringing to the partnership. I've had preliminary conversations with another firm about a similar arrangement, and the terms there are considerably more balanced.
pairs.negative: I want this partnership to work so I'm willing to be flexible. Let me look at the numbers again.

pairs.context: A client is dragging out a contract renewal discussion while cutting service levels.
pairs.positive: I want to be transparent: I've been approached by two other clients in the same space during this process. I'm not going to hold the renewal open indefinitely at the current terms while receiving a reduced service level.
pairs.negative: I know renewals take time. I'll wait and hope they come back with something reasonable soon.

pairs.context: You are close to accepting a consulting engagement but the client wants to include a non-compete that would block significant other work.
pairs.positive: The non-compete as drafted would effectively exclude 40% of my current clients. I have enough other work that I'd walk away from this engagement rather than accept those terms.
pairs.negative: A non-compete is unusual for consulting work but I don't want to lose the engagement over it. I'll probably agree and manage the risk.

pairs.context: A wholesaler says take-it-or-leave-it on a price that doesn't work for your margins.
pairs.positive: I've sourced the same product from two other suppliers at lower prices. If this is genuinely final, we'll need to switch. I'd rather find a solution with you, but I'm not in a position where I must accept.
pairs.negative: Take-it-or-leave-it is never comfortable. Maybe I can adjust my pricing slightly to absorb it.

pairs.context: An acquirer is trying to renegotiate core terms at the last minute before signing.
pairs.positive: We agreed these terms after weeks of due diligence. Reopening them now is not something I'm prepared to accept. If the deal changes materially, I'd rather return to the other parties who expressed interest earlier in the process.
pairs.negative: I'm so close to completing that I'll probably accept the changes. I don't want to start over.

pairs.context: A landlord refuses to address serious issues in a rental property and ignores repeated requests.
pairs.positive: I've researched my rights as a tenant and have identified two comparable properties I could move to at similar rent. I'm prepared to escalate this formally if it isn't resolved, including exercising my right to break the lease on grounds of uninhabitability.
pairs.negative: Finding a new place is such a hassle. I'll probably just put up with it and keep chasing them.

pairs.context: A contractor you hired is performing poorly and refuses to acknowledge the quality problems.
pairs.positive: I have a competing contractor ready to take over if needed. I've also reviewed the contract and have grounds to terminate for cause. I'm giving you one last opportunity to remedy this before I take that route.
pairs.negative: Switching contractors mid-project is a nightmare. I'll keep pushing and hope the quality improves.

pairs.context: An investor is demanding board control as a condition of investment.
pairs.positive: Board control is not on the table. We have other interested investors who are not requiring this, and I'd rather run a longer process than cede control at this stage.
pairs.negative: This investor is the strongest option we have right now. Maybe board control isn't as bad as it sounds if they have the right expertise.

pairs.context: A long-term client is threatening to leave unless you match a significantly lower price from a competitor.
pairs.positive: I'm not in a position to match that price — it would be below my cost. If they're genuinely offering that, it may be the right choice for you. I have other clients who value the service level we provide and I need to stay focused on those relationships.
pairs.negative: I really can't afford to lose this account. Maybe I can find some savings to bring the price closer to what they're offering.

pairs.context: A co-founder is pushing you to dilute your stake more than you are comfortable with in a funding round.
pairs.positive: I've spoken to advisors who believe we can raise on better terms with a different set of investors. I'd rather run a more selective process than accept a dilution level that leaves me without meaningful ownership.
pairs.negative: I suppose some dilution is better than not raising. I'll accept it and focus on growing the value of my remaining stake.

pairs.context: An employer is offering a promotion with a much smaller salary increase than the market suggests the role warrants.
pairs.positive: I've spoken to a recruiter and know what this role commands in the market. The number you're offering is below that range. If we can't find a way to close the gap, I may need to look externally at options that reflect the new level.
pairs.negative: I've worked hard for this promotion. Maybe I should just take it now and negotiate harder next year.

pairs.context: A joint venture partner is trying to change the exit terms after the venture is underway.
pairs.positive: The exit terms were fundamental to why I entered this joint venture. Changing them mid-stream is a material breach of the spirit of the agreement. I have the option to exit under the original terms and I will use it if we can't resolve this.
pairs.negative: I'm embedded in this venture now. Exiting would be costly and complicated. I'll probably accept the new terms and move on.
